fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A value is a term that is fully reduced and cannot be reduced any further. Values are the final results of computations.

It is important to note that the word "value" is overloaded in programming language theory. Syntactically and operationally, a value is a fully reduced term in the syntactic domain characterized by an introduction form and an absence of elimination forms. Semantically and denotationally speaking, a value is an object in the model or the semantic domain of the language. Whereas operationally, the expression \text {add}(2, 3) is different from the expression 5, denotationally, they have the same value, i.e., \llbracket \text {add}(2, 3)\rrbracket = \llbracket 5 \rrbracket .

A neutral term is a normal form that is not a value and cannot be reduced further. In the simply typed lambda calculus, neutral terms are unbound variables, applications of neutral terms to values and elimination forms applied to neutral terms.
A normal form is a term that cannot be reduced any further. In other words, it is a term that does not contain any redexes. A term is a normal form if it is either a value or a neutral term.

The denotational semantics defines the meanings of terms in a language T using a valuation function that maps syntactic terms in the syntactic domain T to semantic values in the semantic domain V. Usually, we enclose expressions in semantic brackets to express their denotation, e.g., for term e, we can write
\llbracket e \rrbracket
such that \llbracket - \rrbracket : T \to V.

The valuation function is compositional; the meaning of a compound term is defined purely in terms of the meanings of its subterms. This compositionality simplifies reasoning, allowing us to replace "equals by equals", i.e., we can substitute terms with other terms having the same meaning.
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket }{\llbracket f(x) \rrbracket = \llbracket f(x') \rrbracket }

Because terms and their semantics are built up inductively, proofs about denotation semantics typically proceed using structural induction. For instance, we can prove that our expression semantics is total: for every expression e in the language, there is a value v such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = v. The proof of totality proceeds by induction on the structure of the expression e.

An elimination form is a term that is reducible by an elimination rule. For instance, \text {Left}\langle n, m\rangle is an elimination form if there is an elimination rule for \text {Left}\langle n, m\rangle . An example of such an elimination rule might be
\frac {\Gamma \vdash \text {Left}\langle n, m\rangle :\text {pair}(\text {nat}, \text {nat})} {\Gamma \vdash n:\text {nat}}
The operational semantics defines the meaning of terms in a langauge T using an execution relation that specifies how terms can be executed in an appropriate machine model.

There are two forms of operations semantics:
small-step operational semantics which describes the individual execution steps
big-step operational semantics which describes the overall results of execution

A small-step semantics for a language ${T} of syntactic terms comprises two components:
a set of execution states S
a transition relation s \rightarrow s' on S that relates each state s to each state s' that may be reached by performaing a single execution step
